coredata.dc:title: Dual-emitting N-doped carbon dots/AuNCs nanohybrids as an efficient ratiometric fluorescent probe and a paper sensor for Pb2+ sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Xinying
Ji, Yayun
Meng, Fanqiang
Tian, Yulu
Wang, Dongmei
Chen, Huan
Gong, Zhengjun
coredata.dc:description: Ratiometric fluorescent sensors could effectively eliminate the interference of spontaneous fluorescence or instrument fluctuation to improve the analysis sensitivity and accuracy. In this work, a dual-emission nitrogen-doped carbon dots/AuNCs (NCDs-AuNCs) nanohybrid was fabricated under mild conditions by the hydrothermal method, without additional coupling agents, which is facile and environmentally friendly. The prepared nanohybrid was employed for the sensitive detection of Pb2+. The sensor exhibited dual emission peaks at 460 and 625 nm with efficient fluorescence resonance energy transfer under a single excitation wavelength of 360 nm, in which the red fluorescence of AuNCs was enhanced and the blue fluorescence of NCDs was slightly quenched with the presence of Pb2+. The quantitative analysis of Pb2+ was performed upon the change of both fluorescence intensities. The sensor was successfully used for Pb2+ detection in real water samples with satisfactory spiking recoveries in a range of 91.0%–101.3% and low relatively standard deviations (RSDs < 3%), which exhibited good selectivity and anti-interference ability. In addition, a paper-based sensor was fabricated by immobilizing the nanohybrids on filter paper, which enables the visual semi-quantitative determination of Pb2+ by the naked eye through the color transition from blue to pink. The prepared NCDs-AuNCs nanohybrids showed great application potential in environmental water monitoring in an efficient and green way.
coredata.dcterms:subject: nanohybrids
fluorescence resonance energy transfer
paper-based sensor
lead (II) ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection mechanism of the ratiometric nanoprobe
Paper-based sensor for visualized Pb2+ detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to express sincere gratitude to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22176154 ) and Science and Technology Department of Sichuan Province (No. 2022YFS0502 , 2022NSFSC0989 & 2022024 ) for their financial support. The authors also greatly thank the Analytical and Testing Center of Southwest Jiaotong University for the help on the TEM characterization of the as-synthesized materials.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM images of (a) NCDs, (b) AuNCs, and (c) NCDs-AuNCs, the inserts are high-resolution TEM.
(a) The FT-IR spectrum of the NCDs, AuNCs, and NCDs-AuNCs; (b) The XPS spectra of NCDs-AuNCs; (c) C1s, (d) O1s, (e) N1s, and (f) Au4f spectra of NCDs-AuNCs.

(a) The UV spectrum and fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs-AuNCs for the detection of Pb2+; (b) Fluorescence emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths; (c) Fluorescence lifetime decay graphs of NCDs, NCDs-AuNCs, and NCDs-AuNCs/Pb2+.

(a) The fluorescence responses of NCDs-AuNCs to different ions; (b) Fluorescence spectra of the NCDs-AuNCs probe in the presence of different concentrations of Pb2+; (c) The fluorescence intensity ratio of F625/F460 versus the concentrations of Pb2+, inset was the calibration curve between F625/F460 and Pb2+ concentration.
Color photograph of the paper-based sensor under different concentrations of Pb2+.
Comparison of the Pb2+ ratio fluorescence sensor with previous ones.

head.ce:title: Dual-emitting N-doped carbon dots/AuNCs nanohybrids as an efficient ratiometric fluorescent probe and a paper sensor for Pb2+ sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu 611756, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State-province Joint Engineering Laboratory of Spatial Information Technology of High-Speed Rail Safety, Chengdu 611756, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Faculty of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu 611756, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The first two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ratiometric fluorescent sensors could effectively eliminate the interference of spontaneous fluorescence or instrument fluctuation to improve the analysis sensitivity and accuracy. In this work, a dual-emission nitrogen-doped carbon dots/AuNCs (NCDs-AuNCs) nanohybrid was fabricated under mild conditions by the hydrothermal method, without additional coupling agents, which is facile and environmentally friendly. The prepared nanohybrid was employed for the sensitive detection of Pb2+. The sensor exhibited dual emission peaks at 460 and 625 nm with efficient fluorescence resonance energy transfer under a single excitation wavelength of 360 nm, in which the red fluorescence of AuNCs was enhanced and the blue fluorescence of NCDs was slightly quenched with the presence of Pb2+. The quantitative analysis of Pb2+ was performed upon the change of both fluorescence intensities. The sensor was successfully used for Pb2+ detection in real water samples with satisfactory spiking recoveries in a range of 91.0%–101.3% and low relatively standard deviations (RSDs < 3%), which exhibited good selectivity and anti-interference ability. In addition, a paper-based sensor was fabricated by immobilizing the nanohybrids on filter paper, which enables the visual semi-quantitative determination of Pb2+ by the naked eye through the color transition from blue to pink. The prepared NCDs-AuNCs nanohybrids showed great application potential in environmental water monitoring in an efficient and green way.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution is becoming more and more severe under the process of human urbanization and industrialization [1]. As a widely existing industrial pollutant, lead is released into the environment from effluent, sludge, and exhaust gas. Excessive exposure to Pb2+ would have negative effects on the nervous system [2], and drinking lead-contaminated water for a long time would also cause organ damage and immune damage [3]. Therefore, it is of great significance to establish a detection method of Pb2+ in water with high sensitivity and selectivity. Up to now, in addition to conventional methods such as atomic absorption spectrometry [4], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [5], and anodic stripping voltammetry [6], fluorescence analysis has been paid much attention due to its convenience and low cost, especially the development of ratiometric fluorescence analysis methods [7].

Ratiometric fluorescent sensors enable the quantitative detection of targets by simultaneously monitoring ratio changes of two fluorescence emission peaks, with built-in correction for environmental interference [8,9]. This method also avoids the influence of factors such as spontaneous fluorescence or instrument fluctuation on a single emission wave probe, thereby achieving more accurate detection results in the analysis of heavy metals. In addition, the response peaks of dual-emission ratio fluorescence sensors are usually in different fluorescence emission bands, and the corresponding fluorescence color is adjustable and variable, allowing semi-quantitative analysis of the target through simple visual identification. This could meet the need for convenient visualization under limited conditions [10,11].

The Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a widespread strategy in dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence detection, with a principle issue being the efficient selection of donor and acceptor. To ensure the overlap between donor emission and acceptor absorption, and achieve stable energy transfer from an excited donor to an acceptor, many synthesis methods of ratiometric fluorescent probes based on organic molecules [12,13] and fluorescent nanomaterials have been reported successively. Among them, fluorescent nanomaterials such as carbon dots (CDs) and nanoclusters (NCs) have been extensively investigated in FRET-based strategies for ratiometric fluorescent probes due to their easy synthesis and low photobleaching properties [14–17]. For example, Niu et al. prepared an efficient ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensing Cd2+ by covalent binding of carbon quantum dots and gold nanoclusters [18]. Li et al. also prepared nanomaterial composite proportional fluorescent probes via carbodiimide as a coupling agent and established a sensing system for the l-ascorbic acid and H2O2 based on the FRET strategy [19]. With the deepening of research on ratiometric fluorescence probes, it is increasingly evident that the covalent binding of double emission wave probes is difficult and time-consuming in the synthesis process. Zhang et al. proposed a new FRET system with covalently linked phthalocyanine-porphyrin (Zn) heterocycles for the detection of Pb2+, which contributes to the further optimization of such FRET systems [20]. However, there are still some gaps in the facile synthesis of the FRET ratio sensor, and more research is needed to synthesize an efficient and rapid FRET ratio sensor.

This work reports a visualized ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on composite nanomaterials of nitrogen-doped carbon dots/AuNCs (NCDs-AuNCs) for the detection of Pb2+. The prepared nanohybrids were characterized to elucidate the fluorescence mechanism. The method employed an efficient FRET effect, which quenched the blue fluorescence of NCDs while enhancing the red fluorescence of AuNCs in the presence of Pb2+, resulting in a change of ratiometric fluorescence. The sensor was successfully applied to the detection of real water samples. To meet the demand for rapid on-site analysis, a visual sensor was fabricated by immobilizing the nanohybrids on filter paper. The evolution of the fluorescence color from blue to pink was displayed instantly with increasing Pb2+ concentration. The developed dual-emission fluorescent nano-sensor has great application prospects in water quality monitoring.

Tetrachloroauric acid trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O, 99 %) and HgCl2 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Citric acid (CA, 99 %), ethylenediamine (EDA, >99 %), and glutathione (GSH, 98 %) were procured from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., ltd (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was used as the solvent of standard solutions in the experiments.

Fluorescence spectra were measured with the FLS1000 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The UV–vis spectra (USB-2000 UV–vis spectrometer, Ocean Optics, USA) of the aqueous solution samples were obtained at 200–700 nm wavelength and 2 nm slit. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained by an FT-IR spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA) in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA) was used to obtain XPS spectra of the fluorescent materials. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were conducted by a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) to determine the morphological distribution characteristics of the fluorescent materials.

For the preparation of NCDs, the citric acid (1 g) and ethylenediamine (1 mL) were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water in a glass conical flask. The aforementioned well-mixed solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 8 h, and stored at 4 °C after cooling to room temperature. To optimize the analytical performance, the influence factors including the molar ratio of citric acid and ethylenediamine (0.2–1), hydrothermal temperature (160, 170, 180, 190, and 200 °C), and reaction time (6, 7, 8, 9, and 10 h) during the preparation of NCDs had been investigated.

The AuNCs were prepared according to the method previously reported with minor modifications [21]. Briefly, 0.48 mL of HAuCl4·3H2O (20 mM) and 20 μL of GSH (0.1 mol/L) were added successively to 4.5 mL ultra-pure water, and it was vigorously stirred at 70 °C for 24 h. The obtained solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min and dialyzed with a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 1000 Da.

The NCDs-AuNCs hybrids were obtained by hydrothermal method with minor modifications. The as-synthesized NCDs (20 μL) was diluted to 4.5 mL ultra-pure water, and then 0.48 mL of HAuCl4·3H2O (20 mM) and 20 μL of GSH (0.1 mol/L) were added successively to the solution, and it was vigorously stirred at 70 °C for 24 h. The obtained yellow–brown solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and dialyzed with a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 1000 Da for 48 h to separate NCDs-AuNCs hybrids. Finally, NCDs-AuNCs hybrids were obtained by freeze-drying for further characterization.

The ratiometric fluorescence detection of Pb2+ was performed by mixing 200 μL of NCDs-AuNCs solution, 200 μL of phosphate buffer saline, and 1.0 mL of artificial water samples and incubating at 25 °C. The fluorescence response intensity at 460 nm (F460) and 625 nm (F625) was separately recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. A calibration curve was plotted with the ratio of F625/F460 versus the Pb2+ concentration. All experiments were repeated three times.

To obtain the best analytical performance, the effects of the volume ratio of NCDs and AuNCs (0.01–0.1), solution pH (3–10), incubation temperatures (15, 25, 35, 45, 55, and 65 °C), and incubation time (3, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, and 60 min) on the Pb2+ sensing were also analyzed. In addition, high concentrations (1 mM) of metal ions and anions were tested for the selectivity of NCDs-AuNCs for Pb2+ (0.1 mM).

The qualitative filter papers were first sliced into a series of circles with a diameter of 7 mm. Then, 2.0 μL of NCDs-AuNCs solution was added dropwise at the center of the filter paper and dried at 50 ℃ for 10 min to form a uniformly colored paper-based sensor. After that, 2.0 μL of different concentrations of Pb2+ solution was eventually dropped into the circular filter paper in the same method as previously mentioned.

The water samples were collected from the lake at Southwest Jiaotong University and tap water in the laboratory (Chengdu, China), respectively. The obtained lake water samples were pretreated by centrifugation for 20 min and filtration with a 0.45 μm membrane filter before the analysis. Then, the water samples were spiked with different concentrations of Pb2+ for spiking recoveries.

TEM was applied to characterize the morphology of the as-synthesized nanomaterials. The NCDs and AuNCs were both well-dispersed nearly-spherical particles, indicating that these materials were highly monodisperse (Fig. 1 a–b). The high-resolution TEM images (HRTEM, the inset of Fig. 1a and b) further indicate the crystallinity of NCDs and AuNCs with a lattice spacing of ca. 0.22 nm and 0.23 nm, respectively, which are indexed to the lattice spacing of the graphene (100) plane and Au (111) facet, respectively [22,23]. The average particle sizes of the prepared NCDs and AuNCs were 2.2 nm and 1.2 nm by judging from 100 random particles, respectively (Fig. S1a–b). The TEM images of the NCDs-AuNCs hybrids (Fig. 1c) showed uniform distribution of particles with well-resolved lattice stripes, indicating that the hybrids still have a good crystal structure and the CDs and AuNCs are in an ordered bound state [21].

The functional groups of NCDs, AuNCs, and NCDs-AuNCs were characterized by FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2 a). The broad absorption bands of NCDs (Fig. 2a–II) at 3319–3516 cm−1 can be explained by the stretching vibrations of OH and NH groups [24], indicating that the surfaces of NCDs are functionalized with amino groups. The bands at 2923 cm−1, 1660 cm−1, and 1400 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibrations of CH [25], CO [26], and CO [27], respectively. The FT-IR spectra of AuNCs are dominated by GSH-related bands (Fig. 2a–I). Since the SH characteristic stretching band of GSH at 2525 cm−1 is not observed in AuNCs and NCDs-AuNCs, this indicates that the thiol group has participated in the reduction process of changing Au3+ to Au0, which resulted in the formation of covalent bonds, indicating the successful synthesis of AuNCs [26]. As shown in Fig. 2a, the infrared absorption band for the CN stretching in NCDs was shifted from 1516 to 1565 cm−1 after the introduction of AuNCs [28], indicating the coordination of AuNCs with the amino groups and amide bonds of NCDs.

The elements in NCDs-AuNCs were investigated by XPS. Four peaks of 532, 401, 285, and 84 eV are displayed in Fig. 2b, which are ascribed to C1s (40.9 %), N1s (10.2 %), O1s (15.4 %), and Au4f (21.1 %), respectively, demonstrating the successful doping of N and Au in the as-synthesized material. The C1s spectrum was shown in Fig. 2c with peaks at 284.8, 286.2, and 288.0 eV, representing the presence of CC/CC, CO/CN, and CO, respectively [26,29]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) showed two peaks of 531.3 and 532.3 eV from the binding energy of the CO and CO groups, respectively[22]. The N1s narrow spectrum (Fig. 2e) can be fitted as two peaks at 399.7 and 401.4 eV corresponding to CN and NH bonds, respectively [22,30]. The Au4f spectrum (Fig. 2f) exhibited two binding energies at 86.9 and 84.0 eV from Au4f5/2 and Au4f7/2, corresponding to two different compositions of Au(0) and Au(I), respectively[18]. This indicated that NCDs-AuNCs still retained the same compositional structure as the individual nanoparticles [31].

To further reveal the optical properties, the corresponding UV–vis absorption spectra were plotted (Fig. S2). The UV absorption spectra of NCDs showed a strong absorption at 350 nm, which can be attributed to the n-π* transition [32]. The UV absorption spectrum of AuNCs had an absorption peak at 250–500 nm and a characteristic peak at around 400 nm [33]. Similarly, the hybridized NCDs-AuNCs had broad absorption peaks recorded at 250–500 nm and showed similar absorption peaks at 350 nm and 400 nm, demonstrating that the obtained NCDs-AuNCs hybrids combine the optical properties of the above two nanomaterials. This demonstrated the successful synthesis of homogeneous binary hybrid nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of the ratiometric nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: To shed light on the ratiometric fluorescence detection mechanism clearly, the fluorescence emission spectra before and after Pb2+ dosing were analyzed, as shown in Fig. 3 a. At the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, two emission peaks at 460 and 625 nm appeared in the fluorescence spectrum of NCDs-AuNCs, corresponding to the blue color of the solution under UV irradiation (the left inset of Fig. 3a). However, the addition of Pb2+ reduced the fluorescence at 460 nm, while the emission peak at 625 nm showed a significant enhancement and the solution rapidly turned red under UV light irradiation (the right inset of Fig. 3a). In addition, the fluorescence emission peak at 460 nm gradually shifted to the right with the increase of excitation wavelength, while the characteristic peak at 625 nm showed an independent red emission characteristic (Fig. 3b). As is well known, the photoluminescent property of CDs was strongly dependent on the particle size [34,35]. The emission peak associated with excitation wavelength indicated that the NCDs prepared are composed of CDs with different sizes (Fig. S1a).

The fluorescence decay kinetics was used to explore the energy transfer between the as-synthesized materials and Pb2+. The fluorescence lifetime decay spectra and the double index fitting comparison data are shown in Fig. 3c and Table S1, respectively. Based on the spectra and the best-fit data, the average lifetime of NCDs was 14.7 ns, while the average lifetime of NCDs-AuNCs after hybridization decreased to 9.45 ns, indicating the existence of electron transfer from NCDs to AuNCs and the FRET effect between AuNCs and NCDs [21,22]. Moreover, there was a weak decay after the addition of Pb2+ with the average lifetime of NCDs-AuNCs of 8.91 ns. It is reported that the FRET effect resulting in a significant increase in fluorescence intensity at 625 nm may be attributed to the porphyrin compounds on the double-emitting NCDs-AuNCs [1,36].

To obtain satisfactory fluorescence properties of NCDs, the effects of three influence factors, i.e., the molar ratio of citric acid and ethylenediamine, hydrothermal temperature, and reaction time during the preparation of NCDs had been investigated. The results demonstrated that the best fluorescence performance was available when the molar ratio of citric acid and ethylenediamine was 1:3 (Fig. S3a), and the hydrothermal time and temperature were 8 h and 180 °C (Fig. S3b and c), which was selected for the following experiment. In addition, the volume ratio of NCDs to chloroauric acid would directly affect the ratiometric fluorescence response signals at 460 and 625 nm for the detection sensitivity of Pb2+. As can be seen from Fig. S3d, the fluorescence efficiency (F625/F460) of NCDs-AuNCs decreased with the increase of VNCDs: Vchloroauric acid from 0.01 to 0.1. The beat fluorescence efficiency was at VNCDs: Vchloroauric acid = 0.03:1 after the addition of Pb2+.

Moreover, the effects of pH, incubation temperature, and incubation time on the detection of Pb2+ by NCDs-AuNCs were investigated to obtain good analytical performance. The phosphate buffer solution with pH in the range of 3–8 was selected for the determination of Pb2+, and the results showed the best fluorescence efficiency at pH = 5 (as shown in Fig. S4a). The effects of different reaction temperatures (15, 25, 35, 45, 55, and 65 °C) and incubation times (3, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, and 60 min) on the Pb2+ analytical performance were also analyzed. The best fluorescence signal could be measured at a room temperature of 25 °C, and the incubation time had little effect on the analytical performance (as shown in Fig. S4b and c).

To clarify the selectivity of NCDs-AuNCs for Pb2+, the fluorescence signal of different kinds of ions, i.e., K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Li+, Cl–, HPO4 –, HCO3 –, NO3 –, CO3 2–, Br–, and SO4 2–, were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the F625/F460 value obtained for the reaction of NCDs-AuNCs with other ions (1 mM) was negligible compared to Pb2+ (0.1 mM), demonstrating the excellent selectivity of the prepared material for Pb2+.

The established analytical method was used to detect Pb2+ in real water samples which were collected from the lake at Southwest Jiaotong University and tap water in the laboratory, respectively. The recoveries were defined as the ratio of detecting concentrations and spiking concentrations in real water samples. As listed in Table 2 , the recoveries were 96.1%–101.3% in tap water and 91.0%–92.3% in lake water, with low relative standard deviation (RSDs, <3 %), indicating the great potential in Pb2+ determination in real samples with high accuracy and reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper-based sensor for visualized Pb2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To perform rapid and portable visual determination in some specific scenarios, we prepared a paper-based dual-emission fluorescent sensor for the visual detection of Pb2+. As presented in Fig. 5 , the paper-based sensor showed a continuous color shift from blue to pink accompanied by an increment of Pb2+ concentration upon exposure to the UV lamp. This visually recognizable color change can be explained by the large separation between the 460 nm and 625 nm emission peaks. In summary, the paper-based dual-emission fluorescent sensor showed certain advantages in terms of portability and stability, especially for some special environments and non-professional field measurements.

In this study, the double-emitting NCDs-AuNCs have been successfully synthesized and applied to the sensing of Pb2+ by ratiometric fluorescence. The ratiometric process is based on the FRET effect between AuNCs and NCDs, which also enables to the improvement of the stability and accuracy of NCDs-AuNCs without additional coupling agents. The prepared sensor exhibited a wide linear range, good selectivity, and high sensitivity to Pb2+, which exhibited the ability to resist environmental interference. Moreover, a paper-based sensor was fabricated, which can be applied to the visual detection of Pb2+ in environmental samples by non-professionals in a fast and green manner. Therefore, the prepared NCDs-AuNCs nanohybrids are a promising candidate for ratio sensing.

Xinying Gong: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yayun Ji: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Fanqiang Meng: Visualization, Methodology. Yulu Tian: Methodology. Dongmei Wang: Funding acquisition, Supervision. Huan Chen: Writing – review & editing. Zhengjun Gong: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to express sincere gratitude to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22176154) and Science and Technology Department of Sichuan Province (No. 2022YFS0502, 2022NSFSC0989 & 2022024) for their financial support. The authors also greatly thank the Analytical and Testing Center of Southwest Jiaotong University for the help on the TEM characterization of the as-synthesized materials.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109535.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
